title: SQL Injection in Login Endpoint
description: User-supplied email is directly interpolated into a raw SQL query string without parameterization. An attacker can craft an email like `' OR 1=1--` to bypass authentication entirely. This flaw enables login as any user including admin.

title: SQL Injection in Product Search
description: The search criteria parameter is concatenated directly into a raw Sequelize SQL query. This allows UNION-based injection to extract arbitrary data from the database schema and all tables. No sanitization or parameterized queries are used.

title: NoSQL Injection via $where in Product Reviews
description: The product ID is concatenated into a MongoDB `$where` clause that evaluates arbitrary JavaScript server-side. An attacker can inject JavaScript expressions to bypass filters or enumerate data. This is equivalent to RCE within the MongoDB engine.

title: NoSQL Injection via $where in Order Tracking
description: The order ID is directly interpolated into a MongoDB `$where` JavaScript expression. Attackers can escape the string context and inject arbitrary JavaScript to access other users' orders. No input sanitization is applied before query execution.

title: NoSQL Injection Allowing Mass Update of Reviews
description: The review update operation uses `multi: true` with an unvalidated `req.body.id` as the filter. An attacker can supply an ID that matches all review documents, overwriting every review in the database. There is no authorization check to ensure the caller owns the review.

title: Remote Code Execution via eval() on Username
description: The application extracts a code payload from a `#{...}` pattern in the username field and passes it directly to `eval()`. Any authenticated user can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the server process context. This provides full server-side code execution.

title: RCE via vm.runInContext with notevil safeEval
description: B2B order data is passed to `safeEval` from the `notevil` library running inside a Node.js VM sandbox. The `notevil` library is known to be bypassable, and the VM sandbox in Node.js is not a security boundary. Attackers can escape to execute arbitrary server-side code.

title: XXE via XML Parsing with External Entities Enabled
description: XML files are parsed with `noent: true` which enables resolution of external XML entities. An attacker can craft an XML file with an entity referencing a local file path (e.g., `/etc/passwd`) or internal network resource. This enables file disclosure and potential SSRF.

title: YAML Bomb / Denial of Service via YAML Parsing
description: Uploaded YAML files are parsed with `js-yaml`'s `load()` function without resource limits on alias expansion. A maliciously crafted YAML file with deeply nested anchors and aliases can cause exponential memory consumption, crashing the server process.

title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key in Source Code
description: A full RSA private key is embedded as a string literal in the source code and used to sign JWT tokens. Anyone with access to the repository can forge valid JWT tokens for any user, including admin. Key rotation is impossible without a code change and redeploy.

title: Password Hashing with MD5 (No Salt)
description: Passwords are hashed using MD5 without any salt. MD5 is cryptographically broken and not suitable for password storage; precomputed rainbow tables can crack most MD5 hashes instantly. The absence of a salt makes hash collisions and dictionary attacks trivial.

title: Hardcoded HMAC Secret for Password Verification
description: A static HMAC secret `pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj` is hardcoded and used for password-related hashing. Static secrets embedded in code are trivially exposed via repository access or binary inspection. Any party knowing the secret can forge HMACs.

title: JWT 'none' Algorithm Accepted
description: The application explicitly handles and accepts JWTs signed with the `none` algorithm, which requires no signature. An attacker can craft a token claiming any user identity without knowing the private key, bypassing all authentication on endpoints that rely on token verification.

title: Algorithm Confusion Attack: HS256 Used to Forge RSA Token
description: The application accepts JWTs for RSA-keyed accounts when signed with HS256 using the public key as the HMAC secret. This is the classic RS256→HS256 algorithm confusion attack. An attacker who obtains the public key can forge valid tokens for any RSA-protected account.

title: Hardcoded Default Credentials in Application Logic
description: Multiple sets of plaintext credentials are hardcoded in the login route, including admin and support accounts with passwords like `admin123`. These credentials are part of challenge logic but expose real working accounts. Any developer with repo access knows these credentials.

title: Weak Cookie Parser Secret
description: The cookie parser is initialized with the trivially guessable secret `kekse`. This secret is used to sign cookies, and knowing it allows an attacker to forge signed cookie values. Any attacker with repo access (or who simply guesses it) can tamper with signed cookies.

title: Admin Role Self-Assignment During Registration
description: The registration endpoint accepts a `role` field in the request body and creates users with whatever role is specified, including `admin`. There is no server-side enforcement that new registrations must receive only the default user role. Any anonymous user can register as an administrator.

title: SSRF via User-Controlled Profile Image URL
description: The application fetches a user-supplied URL to use as a profile image without restricting the URL scheme or target host. An attacker can direct the server to make requests to internal services, cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., `169.254.169.254`), or other internal infrastructure.

title: Path Traversal in Zip File Extraction
description: Zip archive entries are extracted using the entry's internal path joined to the upload directory, with only a `path.resolve()` prefix check that is trivially bypassed using carefully crafted paths. An attacker can write files to arbitrary locations on the filesystem by including `../` sequences in zip entry names.

title: Open Redirect via Allowlist Substring Bypass
description: The redirect allowlist check uses `String.includes()` rather than exact host matching, so a URL like `https://evil.com/https://allowed.example.com` would pass validation. Attackers can craft redirect URLs that contain an allowed domain as a substring while redirecting victims to malicious sites.

title: IP Spoofing via X-Forwarded-For in Rate Limiting
description: The rate limiter key is derived from the client-supplied `X-Forwarded-For` header without verifying whether the application is behind a trusted proxy. An attacker can set this header to any value to bypass per-IP rate limits on sensitive operations like password reset.

title: FTP Directory Listing Publicly Exposed
description: The `/ftp` route mounts a directory index middleware that lists all files in the `ftp/` directory with no authentication required. This exposes internal files, backup archives, and potentially sensitive documents to any unauthenticated visitor.

title: Application Log Files Publicly Accessible
description: Server access logs are served under `/support/logs` with directory listing enabled and no authentication. Log files can reveal internal request paths, user IP addresses, session tokens in query strings, and other operational details useful to attackers for reconnaissance.

title: Prometheus Metrics Endpoint Exposed Without Auth
description: The `/metrics` endpoint serving Prometheus data is accessible to any unauthenticated client. Metrics can reveal request rates, error rates, internal queue depths, memory usage, and other operational data that aids attacker reconnaissance.

title: Overly Permissive CORS Policy
description: CORS is enabled for all origins with `app.use(cors())` using default settings that reflect the request's Origin header. This allows any website to make credentialed cross-origin requests to the API, effectively disabling the Same-Origin Policy protection for all endpoints.

title: No CSRF Protection on State-Changing Endpoints
description: The application does not implement CSRF tokens or SameSite cookie attributes on any state-changing POST/PUT/DELETE endpoints. Combined with the wide-open CORS policy, this allows malicious third-party websites to perform authenticated actions on behalf of logged-in users.

title: Critically Outdated jsonwebtoken (v0.4.0)
description: The application pins `jsonwebtoken` at version `0.4.0`, which is approximately 10+ major versions behind current and contains numerous known CVEs including algorithm confusion vulnerabilities, improper verification, and other signature bypass issues. Current version is 9.x.

title: Critically Outdated express-jwt (v0.1.3)
description: The `express-jwt` middleware is pinned at `0.1.3`, an extremely old version with known authentication bypass vulnerabilities. This middleware handles JWT validation for protected routes; a bypass here grants unauthorized API access.

title: Outdated sanitize-html (v1.4.2) with Bypass Vulnerabilities
description: The `sanitize-html` library is pinned at `1.4.2`, which predates multiple XSS bypass patches. The legacy sanitization path in the application uses this version, meaning user-supplied HTML is not reliably sanitized and stored XSS payloads can persist.

title: Stored XSS via Insufficient HTML Sanitization in Username
description: The username field uses a legacy sanitization function (`sanitizeLegacy`) based on an outdated `sanitize-html` version that has known bypass techniques. A crafted username containing obfuscated XSS payloads can survive sanitization and execute in other users' browsers when the username is rendered.

title: Reflected XSS via Order ID Parameter
description: The order tracking endpoint reflects the raw `id` parameter into the response without encoding. An attacker can craft a URL where the order ID contains an XSS payload like `<iframe src="javascript:alert()">`, which executes in the victim's browser when they visit the crafted link.

title: CSP Injection via Profile Image URL Reflected in Header
description: The user's `profileImage` value is interpolated directly into a Content-Security-Policy header string. An attacker can inject CSP directives by setting a profile image URL containing semicolons and valid CSP keywords, weakening or disabling the page's security policy.

title: unsafe-eval in Content-Security-Policy
description: The Content-Security-Policy for the user profile page includes `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval'`, which explicitly permits the use of `eval()` and `Function()` in scripts. This nullifies the XSS mitigation that CSP is intended to provide for this page.

title: Timing Side-Channel in Product Review Like Endpoint
description: An artificial 150ms delay is introduced in the review like endpoint. While intentional for a challenge, it demonstrates a timing side-channel pattern where response time differences can be exploited to infer information about server-side processing or user state.

title: Sensitive Data Transmitted Without Transport Security Enforcement
description: The application does not enforce HTTPS or set HSTS headers, allowing it to serve traffic over plain HTTP. Credentials, session tokens, and personal data transmitted over HTTP are exposed to network-level eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks.
